refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not from a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can collect energy consumption data (fuel/electrical input) and work output metrics (tonnage moved, distance traveled, or equivalent), compute energy_per_ton, and report the result to stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance) in real time and over the evaluation period. Verify data accuracy against calibrated instrumentation.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure work output (tonnage moved or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute energy efficiency ratio (energy_per_ton) from measured inputs.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall record and report energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders.
refined_fr.note: The refined FR is atomic in intent but requires decomposition into sub-FRs to isolate measurement, computation, and reporting concerns. Each sub-FR is now solution-neutral and independently verifiable.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period

DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation

DP3: Decompose energy consumption into sub-processes (transport, loading, unloading); assign work output proportionally to each; compute energy efficiency per sub-process and aggregate to system level
DP4: Establish energy baseline from historical operational data; measure incremental energy consumption and work output during evaluation period; compute delta energy_per_ton relative to baseline

DP5: Implement dual-path measurement: primary path uses direct instrumentation; secondary path uses operational logs and inference models; cross-validate both to report confidence-bounded energy_per_ton
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation

potential_sources: Sensor placement coupling: energy measurement location (fuel tank, electrical panel, motor) must align with work output measurement location (load cell, GPS, tonnage registry) to avoid scope ambiguity—mitigated by explicit boundary definition in IM layer
Temporal coupling: energy and tonnage data streams must be synchronized to sub-minute granularity; loose coupling achieved via timestamped event streams rather than synchronous RPC
Normalization coupling: energy units (kWh, liters, MJ) and tonnage units (metric tons, payload mass) must be standardized before aggregation; decoupled via schema-driven transformation layer

Validation coupling: acceptance criteria (≤ E_target) depends on evaluation period definition and operational scope; mitigated by parameterizing period and scope in configuration, not hardcoding in logic
coupling_check.notes: DP2 avoids tight coupling by separating concerns: (1) sensor instrumentation (independent per energy/work source), (2) local aggregation (stateless, idempotent), (3) central analytics (single source of truth for ratio computation). Real-time streaming allows independent evolution of sensor networks and analytics logic.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is metered or instrumentable (fuel consumption, electrical draw). RISK: If energy is supplied via shared grid or fuel tank serving multiple operations, attribution to NGHE unit requires sub-metering or allocation model—recommend explicit scope boundary in IM.

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) is directly measurable or derivable from operational logs. RISK: If 'ton_moved' includes partial loads, multi-leg trips, or return journeys, definition ambiguity may inflate or deflate energy_per_ton—recommend operational policy clarification before IM design.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is continuous and uninterrupted. RISK: If operations are seasonal, intermittent, or subject to downtime, energy_per_ton may be skewed by idle consumption—recommend explicit handling of idle vs. active states in DP realization.

ASSUMPTION: Sensors and data infrastructure are reliable and calibrated. RISK: Sensor drift, data loss, or calibration drift over evaluation period may invalidate acceptance criteria—recommend periodic validation checkpoints and fallback to secondary measurement path (DP5 hybrid).

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit real-time instrumentation. RISK: If policies restrict sensor placement or data logging for safety/privacy reasons, DP2 may require redesign—recommend early stakeholder alignment on instrumentation scope.

ASSUMPTION: Central analytics engine has sufficient compute and storage capacity. RISK: High-frequency sensor streams may exceed infrastructure limits—recommend capacity planning and stream sampling strategy in IM design.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement and periodic reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output, computed as energy_used / ton_moved, such that the measured ratio satisfies ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period, with documented scope boundaries for energy attribution (e.g., fuel tank, electrical panel) and work output definition (e.g., loaded tonnage, delivery point), and with validation checkpoints to confirm sensor calibration and data integrity throughout the evaluation period. | RATIONALE: DP2 is selected because it supports real-time visibility (enabling operational feedback), decouples sensor instrumentation from analytics logic (enabling independent scaling and evolution), and provides continuous validation (reducing acceptance-criteria risk). Recommended IM layer should define: (1) energy measurement boundary (which fuel/electrical source), (2) work output measurement boundary (which tonnage registry or load cell), (3) data synchronization protocol (timestamp granularity, latency tolerance), (4) normalization schema (unit conversions, outlier handling), (5) validation checkpoints (calibration schedule, cross-check frequency), and (6) fallback strategy (secondary measurement path or manual audit for high-stakes acceptance decisions).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized real-time energy and tonnage monitoring system encompassing sensor integration, local aggregation logic, streaming pipeline, analytics engine, and continuous KPI computation (energy_per_ton)

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: raw energy and tonnage sensor streams from Vehicle; Outbound: normalized metrics and energy_per_ton KPIs to central analytics and reporting systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational context (mission parameters, load profiles, duty cycles) to calibrate sensor normalization and validation thresholds

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Triggers alerts if energy_per_ton deviates beyond acceptable bounds or sensor anomalies detected during validation

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides reliable streaming transport for sensor data ingestion and metric distribution to remote analytics and reporting endpoints

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural home for energy/tonnage analytics, sensor data aggregation, and KPI computation. All core DP logic (sensor embedding, local aggregation, streaming, analytics, continuous reporting) resides within this boundary. Minor diffusion: Vehicle provides raw sensor hardware; Teleoperation may consume KPI dashboards; Safety Services consumes alerts.

Poor fit: Vehicle is the sensor host (hardware placement), but the DP requires centralized analytics, streaming pipelines, and continuous KPI computation—all of which belong in the analytics/monitoring domain, not the vehicle platform. Placing aggregation and analytics in Vehicle would create tight coupling and duplicate logic across fleet.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/visualization interface, not a data aggregation or analytics engine. The DP's core logic (sensor streaming, normalization, KPI computation) has no cohesion with teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: analytics would leak into control logic.

Poor fit: Safety Services consumes energy_per_ton alerts and thresholds, but does not own the sensor integration, aggregation, or analytics pipeline. The DP's primary responsibility is continuous monitoring and KPI computation, not safety decision-making. Placing analytics here conflates monitoring with safety logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the authoritative owner of real-time energy and tonnage monitoring because it is the designated analytics and KPI computation platform. Sensors are embedded in Vehicle hardware, but the DP's logic—local aggregation, streaming normalization, continuous energy_per_ton calculation, and periodic validation—is a cohesive analytics function that belongs in the central monitoring domain. Vehicle provides sensor data via Telemetry; Safety Services consumes alerts via SafetyAlert; Teleoperation may visualize KPIs via Telemetry.
containment_risks: Sensor hardware lifecycle and calibration ownership must be clearly assigned to Vehicle; NGHE Solution depends on Vehicle providing clean, timestamped sensor streams.
Normalization logic (e.g., temperature, load, duty-cycle adjustments) must be parameterized and versioned in NGHE Solution to avoid tight coupling to Vehicle firmware updates.
Streaming pipeline reliability (Network Link) is critical; latency or packet loss will degrade energy_per_ton accuracy and validation effectiveness.
Periodic validation logic must define clear acceptance criteria and fallback behavior (e.g., alert vs. auto-correct) to avoid ambiguity in Safety Services integration.
If multiple vehicle types or sensor variants exist, NGHE Solution must support polymorphic sensor schemas to avoid diffusion of aggregation logic across Vehicle subtypes.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 directly and completely addresses FR requirement. The DP specifies a coherent solution path: embed sensors → aggregate locally → stream normalized metrics → compute energy_per_ton continuously → validate periodically. This maps cleanly to the functional requirement to 'enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.' The DP is solution-agnostic (does not prescribe specific technologies) and measurable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target).

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: Sensor calibration and hardware lifecycle ownership boundary between Vehicle and NGHE Solution is underspecified. If Vehicle firmware updates sensor scaling factors without NGHE Solution synchronization, normalization logic will diverge and energy_per_ton accuracy will degrade. Recommend explicit versioning contract and change notification protocol.

HIGH: Normalization logic (temperature, load, duty-cycle adjustments) is currently described as residing in NGHE Solution, but if Vehicle provides pre-normalized data, the DP logic diffuses across the Vehicle→NGHE boundary. Clarify whether Vehicle streams raw sensor counts or pre-processed values; if raw, NGHE owns all normalization; if pre-processed, Vehicle owns normalization and NGHE becomes a pass-through aggregator (violates DP intent).

MEDIUM: Periodic validation logic references 'acceptable bounds' and 'sensor anomalies' but does not specify who owns the threshold definition, anomaly detection algorithm, or fallback behavior. If Safety Services defines thresholds independently, validation logic diffuses across NGHE Solution and Safety Services. Recommend NGHE Solution owns validation algorithm; Safety Services owns policy interpretation.

MEDIUM: Streaming pipeline (Network Link) is a critical dependency for DP realization. If network latency or packet loss exceeds design assumptions, energy_per_ton computation becomes stale or incomplete. Recommend explicit SLA for Network Link (e.g., <100ms latency, >99.9% delivery) as a DP precondition.

LOW: If multiple vehicle types or sensor variants exist (e.g., different energy sensors, tonnage measurement methods), NGHE Solution must support polymorphic sensor schemas. Risk: aggregation logic could leak into Vehicle-specific adapters, fragmenting the DP. Recommend NGHE Solution defines a canonical sensor schema and Vehicle adapters are owned by Vehicle module.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM owner with 0.92 containment score. The DP's core logic—local aggregation, streaming normalization, continuous energy_per_ton calculation, and periodic validation—is cohesive and belongs in the central analytics domain. Vehicle correctly provides sensor hardware and raw data streams (0.35 containment reflects that Vehicle is a data source, not an analytics owner). Teleoperation and Safety Services are correct consumers, not owners. However, the DP→IM bridge has three critical weak points: (1) sensor calibration ownership between Vehicle and NGHE, (2) normalization logic boundary (raw vs. pre-processed data), and (3) validation threshold ownership between NGHE and Safety Services. These must be resolved before detailed design to prevent diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly measurable from NGHE Solution outputs. NGHE Solution owns the computation and reporting of energy_per_ton, so verification is traceable to a single module boundary. Recommend: (1) define E_target value and evaluation period in operational requirements, (2) establish data provenance and audit trail for energy and tonnage measurements, (3) define periodic validation acceptance criteria (e.g., sensor drift tolerance, outlier detection thresholds) to ensure energy_per_ton accuracy.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: MEDIUM-HIGH with caveats. Functional allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns measurement, aggregation, and reporting; Vehicle owns sensor hardware; Safety Services owns policy enforcement. However, allocation clarity is degraded by three unresolved boundaries: (1) Sensor calibration and versioning contract between Vehicle and NGHE Solution is implicit, not explicit. Recommend: define a sensor metadata schema (calibration date, scaling factors, firmware version) that Vehicle provides with each data stream, and NGHE Solution validates before aggregation. (2) Normalization logic ownership is ambiguous. Recommend: explicitly state whether Vehicle provides raw sensor counts or pre-normalized values; if raw, NGHE owns all normalization algorithms and parameters; if pre-normalized, Vehicle owns normalization and NGHE owns aggregation only. (3) Validation threshold ownership is unclear. Recommend: NGHE Solution owns the validation algorithm and anomaly detection logic; Safety Services owns the policy for threshold values and alert escalation. Once these three boundaries are formalized in interface specifications, allocation clarity will be HIGH.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (CRITICAL): Add explicit sensor calibration and versioning contract. Specify that Vehicle provides sensor metadata (calibration date, scaling factors, firmware version, timestamp) with each data stream. NGHE Solution validates metadata consistency before aggregation and logs any calibration changes. Define fallback behavior if metadata is missing or inconsistent (e.g., reject stream, use last-known calibration, alert Safety Services).

REVISION 2 (CRITICAL): Clarify normalization logic boundary. Explicitly state in DP: 'Vehicle provides raw sensor counts (energy in Joules, tonnage in kg) with timestamps. NGHE Solution applies normalization transformations (temperature correction, load profile adjustment, duty-cycle weighting) to compute normalized metrics before aggregation.' If Vehicle is to provide pre-normalized data, revise DP to reflect that and move normalization ownership to Vehicle.

REVISION 3 (CRITICAL): Formalize validation logic ownership and acceptance criteria. Specify: 'NGHE Solution owns periodic validation algorithm (e.g., statistical outlier detection, sensor drift estimation). Validation compares energy_per_ton against configurable bounds and flags anomalies. Safety Services owns threshold policy (e.g., alert if energy_per_ton exceeds E_target by >10% for >5 consecutive minutes). NGHE Solution streams validation results to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.' Define acceptance criteria for validation (e.g., false positive rate <1%, detection latency <30 seconds).

REVISION 4 (HIGH): Add Network Link SLA as a DP precondition. Specify: 'Streaming pipeline must deliver sensor data with <100ms latency and >99.9% packet delivery rate to ensure energy_per_ton accuracy within ±5% tolerance. If Network Link SLA is violated, NGHE Solution enters degraded mode: use cached normalization parameters and alert Safety Services of potential data staleness.'

REVISION 5 (HIGH): Define polymorphic sensor schema for multi-vehicle support. If fleet includes multiple vehicle types, specify: 'NGHE Solution defines a canonical sensor schema (energy_source, energy_unit, tonnage_source, tonnage_unit, timestamp, metadata). Vehicle adapters (owned by Vehicle module) map vehicle-specific sensor outputs to canonical schema. NGHE Solution aggregates only canonical schema instances, avoiding vehicle-specific logic diffusion.'

REVISION 6 (MEDIUM): Specify data provenance and audit trail requirements. Recommend: 'NGHE Solution maintains immutable log of all energy and tonnage measurements, normalization parameters, and validation results. Log includes source (Vehicle ID, sensor ID), timestamp, raw value, normalized value, and validation status. Log is accessible to Safety Services and external auditors for verification of energy_per_ton compliance.'

REVISION 7 (MEDIUM): Define periodic validation schedule and acceptance criteria. Specify: 'Periodic validation occurs at [daily/weekly/monthly] intervals. Validation compares energy_per_ton against historical baseline and flags if deviation exceeds [threshold]%. If validation fails, NGHE Solution generates incident report and alerts Safety Services. Acceptance criteria: validation must complete within [time window] and achieve [confidence level] before energy_per_ton is reported as valid.'

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass logically, but the DP-IM gate reveals CRITICAL and HIGH diffusion risks that violate the axiomatic design principle of independence and clarity. The core issue is that three boundary conditions between NGHE Solution, Vehicle, and Safety Services are underspecified: (1) sensor calibration ownership and versioning contract, (2) normalization logic boundary (raw vs. pre-processed data), and (3) validation threshold ownership. These ambiguities will cause logic to diffuse across module boundaries during detailed design, degrading modularity and creating maintenance/verification nightmares. Given the HIGH safety criticality of energy measurement, proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without resolving these boundaries is premature and risky. The DP must be revised to explicitly formalize these three interface contracts before decomposition proceeds. This is a REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL decision, not STOP, because the DP is fundamentally sound—it just needs explicit boundary clarification to be implementable.

revise.why: DP2 must be revised to explicitly specify three critical interface contracts that are currently implicit: (1) Sensor Calibration & Versioning Contract: Vehicle provides sensor metadata (calibration date, scaling factors, firmware version, timestamp) with each data stream; NGHE Solution validates metadata consistency and logs calibration changes; define fallback behavior for missing/inconsistent metadata. (2) Normalization Logic Boundary: Explicitly state that Vehicle provides raw sensor counts (energy in Joules, tonnage in kg) with timestamps, and NGHE Solution applies all normalization transformations (temperature correction, load profile adjustment, duty-cycle weighting); if Vehicle is to provide pre-normalized data, revise DP accordingly and move normalization ownership to Vehicle. (3) Validation Threshold Ownership: NGHE Solution owns periodic validation algorithm (statistical outlier detection, sensor drift estimation) and streams validation results to Safety Services; Safety Services owns threshold policy (alert criteria, escalation rules); define acceptance criteria for validation (false positive rate, detection latency). Additionally, add Network Link SLA as a DP precondition (<100ms latency, >99.9% delivery) and define polymorphic sensor schema for multi-vehicle support. These revisions transform DP2 from a logically correct but architecturally ambiguous specification into a clear, implementable design with explicit module boundaries and interface contracts.

assumptions: Vehicle firmware and NGHE Solution can be versioned and synchronized independently; a formal change notification protocol exists between Vehicle and NGHE Solution teams.
Network Link provides sufficient bandwidth and latency to stream raw sensor data from Vehicle to NGHE Solution without significant buffering or staleness.
Safety Services has authority to define validation thresholds and alert policies; NGHE Solution is a data provider, not a policy enforcer.
Energy and tonnage sensors on Vehicle are calibrated at known intervals and calibration metadata can be embedded in data streams.
The evaluation period for energy_per_ton compliance is defined operationally (e.g., per shift, per route, per month) and is known before detailed design.
Multiple vehicle types or sensor variants may exist in the fleet; NGHE Solution must support polymorphic sensor schemas.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Sensor calibration drift between Vehicle firmware updates and NGHE Solution normalization logic could cause energy_per_ton accuracy to degrade undetected. Mitigation: formalize sensor metadata schema and versioning contract; implement validation checks in NGHE Solution to detect calibration inconsistencies.

CRITICAL: Normalization logic boundary is ambiguous. If Vehicle provides pre-normalized data, NGHE Solution becomes a pass-through aggregator and the DP intent (continuous energy_per_ton computation with normalization) is violated. Mitigation: explicitly specify in revised DP whether Vehicle provides raw or pre-normalized data; if raw, NGHE owns all normalization; if pre-normalized, revise DP to reflect aggregation-only role.

HIGH: Validation threshold ownership is unclear. If Safety Services and NGHE Solution define thresholds independently, validation logic will diffuse and anomaly detection will be inconsistent. Mitigation: formalize that NGHE Solution owns validation algorithm; Safety Services owns threshold policy and alert escalation.

HIGH: Network Link SLA is not specified. If latency exceeds design assumptions, energy_per_ton computation becomes stale and accuracy degrades. Mitigation: add explicit SLA precondition to DP (<100ms latency, >99.9% delivery); define degraded-mode behavior if SLA is violated.

MEDIUM: Periodic validation schedule and acceptance criteria are not defined. If validation is infrequent or acceptance criteria are loose, sensor anomalies may go undetected for extended periods. Mitigation: specify validation frequency (daily/weekly/monthly) and acceptance criteria (confidence level, time window) in revised DP.

MEDIUM: Data provenance and audit trail requirements are not specified. If energy_per_ton measurements cannot be traced to source sensors and normalization parameters, verification and compliance auditing will be difficult. Mitigation: add requirement that NGHE Solution maintains immutable log of all measurements, normalization parameters, and validation results.

LOW: If fleet includes multiple vehicle types with different sensor schemas, NGHE Solution must support polymorphic aggregation. Risk: aggregation logic could leak into vehicle-specific adapters, fragmenting the DP. Mitigation: define canonical sensor schema in NGHE Solution; Vehicle adapters (owned by Vehicle module) map vehicle-specific outputs to canonical schema.
evidence_needed: Formal sensor metadata schema specification (calibration date, scaling factors, firmware version, timestamp) that Vehicle provides with each data stream.

Clarification document: does Vehicle provide raw sensor counts or pre-normalized values? If raw, what normalization transformations does NGHE Solution apply? If pre-normalized, what is Vehicle's normalization algorithm and how is it versioned?

Interface specification between NGHE Solution and Safety Services: what validation results does NGHE Solution stream? What threshold policy does Safety Services enforce? What is the alert escalation protocol?
Network Link SLA specification: latency, packet delivery rate, bandwidth, and failure modes. Define degraded-mode behavior if SLA is violated.
Operational requirements: what is E_target value? What is the evaluation period (per shift, per route, per month)? What is the acceptable tolerance for energy_per_ton accuracy (±5%, ±10%)?
Periodic validation schedule and acceptance criteria: how often is validation performed? What is the false positive rate tolerance? What is the detection latency requirement?
Data provenance and audit trail specification: what information must be logged? How long is the log retained? Who has access to the log?
Fleet composition and sensor variant analysis: how many vehicle types exist? What are the sensor schema differences? How should NGHE Solution handle polymorphic schemas?
